text: Last class we read the subtext and emphasized exactly what mattered.
Today we review a failure and explain what should have happened.

If we had used dual-sourcing, we would not be facing a stockout now.
If our forecasting were better, we would not have missed the demand spike.
If we had run a post-mortem sooner, we would know the root cause today.
If the supplier were reliable, the line would not have stopped last week.
Let us be honest in this post-mortem. One supplier with no backup was a single point of failure. If we had used dual-sourcing, we would not be down a line today.
Agreed. The stockout landed during a demand spike, so the fallout was enormous. In hindsight, the signs were there for weeks.
Right. If our forecasting were sharper, we would have caught that spike. The stopgap only bought two days before the ripple effect spread.
So what is the corrective action? If we had carried safety stock, the line would not have stopped at all.
Exactly. Dual-sourcing on critical parts, a real safety buffer, and a full overhaul of how we forecast. That builds resilience.
Good. If we lock this in now, there will be no recurrence. Little did I expect such a clear plan from one post-mortem.
If we had used dual-sourcing, we would not be down a line now.
One supplier with no backup was a single point of failure.
Our corrective action is safety stock and a full overhaul.
Let us run an honest post-mortem and prevent a recurrence.

Hi Fabiana, it is Klaus. Before the post-mortem this afternoon, here is the short version of what went wrong. A single supplier failed, and because that supplier was our only one, it became a single point of failure. If we had used dual-sourcing, we would not be staring at an empty line today. The stockout hit during a demand spike, so the fallout was huge. In hindsight, the warning signs were there for weeks. If our forecasting were sharper, we would have spotted the spike in time. We had no safety stock, only a thin stopgap that bought us two days. The ripple effect reached three regions. A full overhaul of the supplier list is on the table. So the post-mortem is not about blame. It is about foresight: what corrective action builds real resilience, and how we stop a recurrence. Come ready to be honest. Talk soon.

Hi team, here is my read after the post-mortem. It was honest, and that mattered. The root issue was a single point of failure: one supplier, no backup. If we had built safety stock last quarter, we would not be scrambling now. And if our process were any stronger, the line would not have stopped at all. In hindsight, the demand spike was predictable, so this is really a foresight problem. The fallout was a ripple effect across three regions, far beyond a quick stopgap. Our corrective action is clear: dual-sourcing on critical parts, a real safety buffer, and a full overhaul of how we forecast. That is what builds resilience and prevents a recurrence. If I had pushed for this a year ago, today would look very different. I will send the lessons-learned document by Friday.

A single supplier fails and the line stops completely. The stockout hits during a demand spike, and the fallout spreads across three regions. Klaus and Fabiana run an honest post-mortem to find the real cause. They agree the corrective action is dual-sourcing, safety stock, and a full overhaul. Fabiana writes the lessons-learned document to prevent any recurrence.

Dual-sourcing protects us from one supplier failing.

In hindsight, the demand spike was obvious.
Our corrective action is dual-sourcing and safety stock.
If we had used dual-sourcing, we would not be down a line now.
